original_abstract: Efficient C–C bond cleavage and the complete oxidation of alcohols to CO₂ are fundamental challenges in developing high-performance direct alcohol fuel cells for renewable energy applications. In this work, we report the synthesis of core/shell Au/Pt nanowires (NWs) featuring stepped Pt clusters deposited along ultrathin (2.3 nm), stepped Au NW templates. This unique architecture serves as a robust catalyst for the effective oxidation of alcohols. We found that the catalytic performance is highly sensitive to the Au/Pt ratio; specifically, the Au₁.₀/Pt₀.₂ NWs—which possess the highest density of stepped Au/Pt sites (~75%)—exhibit exceptional activity for ethanol electro-oxidation, achieving an unprecedented mass activity of 196.9 A/mgₚₜ (32.5 A/mgₚₜ₊ₐᵤ). Beyond ethanol, these NWs are highly effective at catalyzing the oxidation of other primary alcohols, including methanol, n-propanol, and ethylene glycol. Using in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and infrared reflection-absorption spectroscopy (IRRAS), we characterized the catalyst’s structural evolution and identified key reaction intermediates. These measurements provide direct evidence that the synergy between low-coordinated Pt sites and the stepped Au NW core is essential for facilitating the reaction. Furthermore, DFT calculations confirm that this under-coordinated Pt–Au surface significantly lowers the energy barrier for C–C bond cleavage. Our findings establish a general design strategy for core/shell catalysts with stepped surfaces, offering a pathway toward more efficient electro-oxidation reactions and the advancement of sustainable fuel cell technologies.
humanized_abstract: Cleavage of C–C bonds in alcohols and their complete oxidation to CO 2 are among the bottlenecks in developing direct alcohol fuel cells (DAFCs) that can be efficiently powered by renewable energy sources. Here, we demonstrated a facile strategy to synthesize core/shell Au/Pt nanowires (NWs) consisting of stepped Pt clusters supported on ultrathin (2.3 nm), single-crystal, and stepped Au NWs. The as-obtained Au/Pt NWs behave as well as the Au/Pt NWs treated in a H 2 atmosphere catalyzed the oxidation of alcohols efficiently. Notably, the catalytic performance of Au 1.0 /Pt 0.2 NWs with the highest density of stepped Au/Pt sites (approximately 75%) reached a mass activity of 196.9 A/mg Pt (32.5 A/mg Pt au ) toward ethanol oxidation, which is the highest value reported for DAFCs. In addition to ethanol, other primary alcohols, such as methanol, n-propanol, and ethylene glycol, were also catalyzed efficiently on the Au/Pt NWs. The structural evolution of the catalysts was identified by in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and the reaction intermediates were identified by in situ infrared reflection-absorption spectroscopy (IRRAS). Collectively, these results demonstrate that the synergy between low-coordinated sites on Pt and the stepped Au core of the NWs is crucial for achieving high activity toward the catalytic oxidation of alcohols. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations revealed that the under-coordinated Pt-Au surface can greatly reduce the energy barrier for the cleavage of C–C bond in alcohols. Therefore, our results provide a general design strategy for the preparation of core/shell catalysts with stepped surfaces for the efficient catalytic oxidation of alcohols and development of high-performance DAFCs.
